_comment: Curated JA4 -> known client, from the tools/fingerprint-matrix ground-truth runs (each client x base image against tls.peet.ws, cross-checked against this project's own engine). A JA4's ja4_b tracks the OpenSSL build (so it shifts by base image) and ja4_c tracks the library; labels name both. httpx and aiohttp share Python's ssl defaults and collapse to one JA4. Extend by running tools/fingerprint-matrix.

t13d301300_1d37bd780c83_5ae85263eb3b.name: Python urllib / Ruby net/http (stdlib)
t13d301300_1d37bd780c83_5ae85263eb3b.env: OpenSSL stdlib defaults, no ALPN — Python urllib and Ruby's Net::HTTP collapse to one JA4

t13d1712h1_ab0a1bf427ad_882d495ac381.name: Python requests
t13d1712h1_ab0a1bf427ad_882d495ac381.env: Python 3.10–3.13, standalone OpenSSL 3 — Alpine (musl), uv, or macOS-arm; the OS never enters the ClientHello

t13d1712h1_ab0a1bf427ad_8e6e362c5eac.name: Python httpx / aiohttp
t13d1712h1_ab0a1bf427ad_8e6e362c5eac.env: Python 3.10–3.13, standalone OpenSSL 3 — Alpine (musl), uv, or macOS-arm

t13d1713h1_ab0a1bf427ad_89ab6efea773.name: Python urllib (stdlib)
t13d1713h1_ab0a1bf427ad_89ab6efea773.env: Python 3.10–3.13, standalone OpenSSL 3 — Alpine (musl), uv, or macOS-arm

t13d3012h1_1d37bd780c83_882d495ac381.name: Python requests
t13d3012h1_1d37bd780c83_882d495ac381.env: Python 3.9, standalone OpenSSL 3.5 (uv / macOS-arm) — 3.9's wider legacy cipher default

t13d1812h1_85036bcba153_b26ce05bbdd6.name: Python requests / urllib3
t13d1812h1_85036bcba153_b26ce05bbdd6.env: official CPython build (OpenSSL 3.0.x — Windows / macOS / uv); ja4_b is the build, ja4_c b26ce05bbdd6 is urllib3's own SSLContext (post_handshake_auth, no session ticket) — httpx on the same build differs by that one extension. First seen as Windows 10, Python 3.14.

t13d591000_a33745022dd6_1f22a2ca17c4.name: Node.js https / axios / got (no ALPN)
t13d591000_a33745022dd6_1f22a2ca17c4.env: Node 18–20 — bundled OpenSSL 3 (same on Debian & Alpine)

t13d5911h1_a33745022dd6_1f22a2ca17c4.name: Node.js fetch / undici
t13d5911h1_a33745022dd6_1f22a2ca17c4.env: Node 18–20 — bundled OpenSSL 3 (same on Debian & Alpine)

t13d1911h2_9dc949149365_e7c285222651.name: Go net/http
t13d1911h2_9dc949149365_e7c285222651.env: Go 1.21–1.23, CGO / go run — pure-Go TLS, same on any base image

t13d1910h2_9dc949149365_97f8aa674fd9.name: Go net/http
t13d1910h2_9dc949149365_97f8aa674fd9.env: Go 1.14–1.20 — pure-Go TLS 1.3; cipher list 9dc949149365 is stable across the whole 1.13–1.23 range, only ja4_c moves

t13d1911h2_9dc949149365_d811adc85aab.name: Go net/http
t13d1911h2_9dc949149365_d811adc85aab.env: Go 1.13 — pure-Go TLS 1.3 (11 extensions; distinct ja4_c from 1.14+)

t12d1610h2_8cdfa2d4673b_a6913cb1f049.name: Go net/http
t12d1610h2_8cdfa2d4673b_a6913cb1f049.env: Go 1.12 — TLS 1.2 default (1.13 made TLS 1.3 the default); a different cipher list

t13d1411h2_cbb2034c60b8_e7c285222651.name: Go net/http
t13d1411h2_cbb2034c60b8_e7c285222651.env: Go 1.21–1.23, static build (CGO off) — distroless/scratch; a different cipher set

_java: Java TLS is JSSE (pure Java), so the base image is irrelevant — identical on Debian and Alpine — and the JDK major sets the fingerprint. java.net.http and Apache HttpClient5 both use the JDK's default cipher list (same ja4_b 1d947a95fc68); Apache classic offers no ALPN, so its ja4_a lacks h2. OkHttp overrides the cipher list with its own restricted set (ja4_b 5e2a75874763), so it does NOT collapse onto the JDK default.

t13d3112h2_1d947a95fc68_b72e173852fa.name: Java java.net.http (stdlib)
t13d3112h2_1d947a95fc68_b72e173852fa.env: JDK 11 — pure-Java JSSE, same on any base image

t13d3113h2_1d947a95fc68_7e1102d2036b.name: Java java.net.http (stdlib)
t13d3113h2_1d947a95fc68_7e1102d2036b.env: JDK 17–21 — pure-Java JSSE, same on any base image

t13d311100_1d947a95fc68_b72e173852fa.name: Java JSSE, no ALPN (SSLSocket / Apache HttpClient5 / HttpURLConnection)
t13d311100_1d947a95fc68_b72e173852fa.env: JDK 11 — bare JSSE, no ALPN; captured raw SSLSocket matches this exactly. ja4_c is java.net.http's too (JA4 excludes ALPN/SNI from ja4_c), so those differ only in ja4_a. Same on any base image.

t13d311200_1d947a95fc68_7e1102d2036b.name: Java JSSE, no ALPN (SSLSocket / Apache HttpClient5 / HttpURLConnection)
t13d311200_1d947a95fc68_7e1102d2036b.env: JDK 17–21 — bare JSSE, no ALPN; raw SSLSocket matches exactly. Same on any base image.

t13d1113h2_5e2a75874763_a75264be95d7.name: Java OkHttp
t13d1113h2_5e2a75874763_a75264be95d7.env: JDK 11 — OkHttp's own restricted cipher list; same on any base image

t13d1114h2_5e2a75874763_89e9e4bb3419.name: Java OkHttp
t13d1114h2_5e2a75874763_89e9e4bb3419.env: JDK 17–21 — OkHttp's own restricted cipher list; same on any base image

_dotnet: The .NET HttpClient (SocketsHttpHandler) drives the handshake through the OS OpenSSL, so — like Python — the base image's OpenSSL sets ja4_b and the fingerprint shifts by base: .NET 8 fingerprints differently on Debian and Alpine.
_rust: Rust's axis is the TLS backend, not the base image. reqwest/ureq built with rustls (pure Rust) fingerprint identically on Debian and Alpine — base-independent, like Go. reqwest's default native-tls is OpenSSL on Linux, so it shifts by base: Debian and Alpine differ.

t13d1011h1_61a7ad8aa9b6_3fcd1a44f3e3.name: Rust reqwest (rustls)
t13d1011h1_61a7ad8aa9b6_3fcd1a44f3e3.env: rustls — pure-Rust TLS, same on any base image

t13d101000_61a7ad8aa9b6_3fcd1a44f3e3.name: Rust ureq (rustls)
t13d101000_61a7ad8aa9b6_3fcd1a44f3e3.env: rustls — pure-Rust TLS, no ALPN; same on any base image

_php: PHP drives TLS through the OS OpenSSL on Linux, so ext-curl shifts by base image (Debian vs Alpine/musl), like Python and .NET. Two distinct TLS paths: ext-curl (libcurl — the default handler under Guzzle and Symfony HttpClient) offers h2 and a 30-cipher list; the native stream wrapper (file_get_contents / stream_socket_client) offers no ALPN and a wide 68-cipher list and is stable across PHP 8.1-8.4 and base image.

t13d681100_13e0e9e1c501_8e6e362c5eac.name: PHP streams (file_get_contents)
t13d681100_13e0e9e1c501_8e6e362c5eac.env: PHP 8.1-8.4, OpenSSL 3.5 — same on Debian & Alpine; no ALPN, wide cipher list

_dart: Dart/Flutter bundle BoringSSL statically, so the base image is irrelevant (like Go and rustls) and the Dart SDK version sets the cipher list. dart:io's HttpClient (also package:http and dio, which wrap it) is HTTP/1.1, so it offers no ALPN. These two exact JA4s are dart:io; the wider ja4_b 5b57614c22b0 cluster also carries h2/http1.1 variants — Flutter apps using an h2-capable client — not yet pinned to an exact build.

t13d171000_5b57614c22b0_78e6aca7449b.name: Dart dart:io HttpClient (package:http / dio)
t13d171000_5b57614c22b0_78e6aca7449b.env: Dart 3.5–3.12, BoringSSL — ja4_b 5b57614c22b0 is the mobile-BoringSSL cipher list it shares with Android Conscrypt; no ALPN

t13d181000_e8a523a41297_78e6aca7449b.name: Dart dart:io HttpClient (package:http / dio)
t13d181000_e8a523a41297_78e6aca7449b.env: Dart 3.3, BoringSSL (bundled) — older cipher set; no ALPN

_android: Android's system TLS is Conscrypt (Google's BoringSSL provider), so native apps — Google Play Services, Firebase, GCM, and anything on OkHttp/HttpURLConnection — share ja4_b 5b57614c22b0, the same mobile-BoringSSL cipher list Flutter/Dart carries; the ja4_c then varies by component and Android version. Android's WebView and Chrome are Chromium, so they use Chrome's ja4_b 8daaf6152771 instead. These full JA4s are ground truth from a labelled Android capture that also matches the live corpus.

t13d1712h2_5b57614c22b0_3f5d972527c0.name: Android — Google Play Services (Conscrypt)
t13d1712h2_5b57614c22b0_3f5d972527c0.env: Android system TLS (Conscrypt/BoringSSL); h2 — android.googleapis.com, digitalassetlinks

t13d171100_5b57614c22b0_3f5d972527c0.name: Android — Google Play Services (Conscrypt)
t13d171100_5b57614c22b0_3f5d972527c0.env: Android system TLS (Conscrypt/BoringSSL); no ALPN — android.googleapis.com

t13d1713h1_5b57614c22b0_eca864cca44a.name: Android — Firebase / Google services (Conscrypt)
t13d1713h1_5b57614c22b0_eca864cca44a.env: Android system TLS (Conscrypt/BoringSSL); h1 — firebaseinstallations, clientservices

t13d171200_5b57614c22b0_eca864cca44a.name: Android — Firebase / GCM (Conscrypt)
t13d171200_5b57614c22b0_eca864cca44a.env: Android system TLS (Conscrypt/BoringSSL); no ALPN — mtalk.google.com (push)

t13d1714h1_5b57614c22b0_7baf387fc6ff.name: Android — Firebase / Play (Conscrypt)
t13d1714h1_5b57614c22b0_7baf387fc6ff.env: Android system TLS (Conscrypt/BoringSSL); h1 — firebaseremoteconfig, play.googleapis.com

_mobile_apps: Mobile apps with their own TLS stack, named from the SNIs they phone home to. ByteDance's apps use 'ttnet', a deliberately minimal (9-cipher) BoringSSL config with its mssdk device-attestation — the same shape across CapCut and TikTok. Keyed on the full JA4, since the 9-cipher list is shared with a few other minimal-config clients.

t13d0913h1_f91f431d341e_7dc0f4860127.name: ByteDance app (CapCut / TikTok — ttnet)
t13d0913h1_f91f431d341e_7dc0f4860127.env: minimal-cipher mobile TLS (9 ciphers, ttnet + mssdk); capcutapi.com

_curl: curl / libcurl is not one fingerprint: it is the OS OpenSSL's cipher list (so it shifts by distro and OpenSSL version) plus curl's own extensions, and it offers h2 by default (h1 with --http1.1). These name curl across the major distro families — the same libcurl builds sit behind PHP's ext-curl, Guzzle and Symfony HttpClient. RedHat's system crypto-policy gives Fedora/RHEL a cipher list of their own, shared with Amazon Linux 2023. In the wild the exact JA4 varies by patch build, so these match by full JA4, not the whole ja4_b family.

t13d3513h2_bfa337485184_8d7351bd871b.name: curl / libcurl
t13d3513h2_bfa337485184_8d7351bd871b.env: Fedora — RedHat crypto-policy (OpenSSL 3); shares the cipher list with Amazon Linux 2023; h2

_scripting: Other stdlib scripting clients. GNU wget links GnuTLS on Debian, not OpenSSL, so its cipher list (ja4_b 723694b0fccc) is unlike every OpenSSL client here. Perl's HTTP::Tiny / LWP go through IO::Socket::SSL over OpenSSL. Both are HTTP/1.1, no ALPN.

t13d291300_723694b0fccc_148158566ece.name: wget (GNU, GnuTLS)
t13d291300_723694b0fccc_148158566ece.env: Debian — GnuTLS, not OpenSSL; no ALPN

_browsers: Keys below are BARE ja4_b cipher-list hashes (12 hex, no ja4_a, no underscore): a cipher list that is a client's signature across the extension permutations and versions that shift ja4_a and ja4_c. A bare-ja4_b match beats the a_b prefix (which catches only one extension count). An entry with an `alpn` list is a BROWSER — the cipher list is only that browser when the full ALPN is a browser's (h2, http/1.1, optionally behind h3), because impersonation tools (curl-impersonate, uTLS) copy the ciphers but not the ALPN, and JA4 can't tell 'h2' alone from 'h2, http/1.1'. An entry with NO `alpn` list is a PLATFORM (Android Conscrypt / Flutter's mobile BoringSSL, or Apple SecureTransport) whose browser AND native apps legitimately offer every ALPN, and whose cipher list is distinctive enough to identify without the ALPN guard. Keyed only for cipher lists distinctive to one client — never a shared OpenSSL build.

8daaf6152771.name: Google Chrome / Chromium
8daaf6152771.env: Chromium cipher-suite signature — Chrome, Edge, Brave, Opera; any extension permutation or version

86a278354501.name: Mozilla Firefox
86a278354501.env: Firefox cipher-suite signature — also forks (Zen, LibreWolf, Tor Browser); any extension permutation or version

a09f3c656075.name: Apple Safari / WebKit
a09f3c656075.env: Apple SecureTransport cipher-suite signature — Safari (macOS/iOS), WKWebView, and native URLSession apps; the 3DES legacy tail (0xC008/0xC012/0x000A) is Apple's tell, no other modern stack carries it. Platform entry like Android's Conscrypt: Apple system TLS legitimately offers any ALPN (h2, http/1.1, dot, none), so the cipher list alone is the signature — not ALPN-gated

5b57614c22b0.name: Android (Conscrypt) / Flutter (BoringSSL)
5b57614c22b0.env: mobile-BoringSSL cipher-suite signature — Android system TLS (Play Services, Firebase, OkHttp apps) and Flutter/Dart; any ALPN
